display_name: Updating a Systematic Literature Review on Knowledge Management Diagnostics in Software Development Organizations

abstract: It is our great pleasure to introduce the latest special issue of the Concurrency and Computation: Practice and Experience. The issue contains the results of the latest research in the wide-ranging topics regarding computers and computational science. The contemporary problems tackled in the issue concern information technology and are connected not only with improving software and hardware but also with human-machine interfaces. Especially, this last issue is current and up-to-date as computer technologies interlocated between human-supporting machinery are a natural step in developing our civilization. The topics presented in the issue include the problems relating to modeling new computational system, monitoring health, system diagnostics, and algorithms for big data management. We are sure that every reader interested in computational engineering might find some very interesting information. Fast improvement in modeling and numerical methods made it possible to apply some of the solutions in practical implementations. For example, systems aiding and assisting in navigation aiming to provide safety and traffic optimization have become very popular in a modern vehicular technology. Vochin et al1 outlined the contemporary situation related to significant traffic increase and congestion events in big cities, complex road infrastructure, and road hazardous conditions, simultaneously proposing an innovative navigation system solution gathering traffic data acquired through car sensors and security systems. They draw up concepts, architecture, design, and embedded economic implementation for a quasi-real-time kernel solution. Sulikowski et al2 proposed an eyeless tracking tool of human-website interaction. The solution can be used in web ergonomics enhancement systems, content adaptation, or recommender systems. The decision-tree classification and random forest approaches were used for a usability results presentation. Another dynamic information foraging approach was proposed in the work of Drias and Kechid,3 where game theory was used. The authors described a game played by a set of self-interested agents reaching web pages, optimized in terms of shortest time possible. They used pure, mixed, and fully mixed strategies for the agents allowing to effectively get information from the web. The dynamic aspect of the information foraging approach was discussed and the Citation Network Dataset including over 2 million scientific publications was used as a representative example. Mejía et al4 described the Software Process Improvement initiative improving software processes through the evaluation of the current organizational practices, competencies, and experiences. Jarzębowska et al5 projected a new automated generation method of dynamic models taking into account friction and damping as well as exposing to position or kinematic constraints. The method was applied to a manipulator model, including predefined end-effector performance. Additionally, manipulator constrained motion was simulated. Robotic applications are very often connected with lean production, where optimization of production, cost effectiveness, and waste reduction are three of the most important issues. The case study by Bragança et al6 following the CRISP-DM methodology was presented in order to create a model able to reduce wastage. Regression algorithms were induced to predict target results basing on RandomTree algorithm, average data grouping, and average method class creation. The scientific works presented in this special issue provide insights in fields related to modeling new computational system, monitoring health, system diagnostics, and algorithms for big data management. We are sure that every reader interested in computational engineering might find some very interesting information. Leszek Chybowski is an associate professor at Maritime University of Szczecin and an Editor in Chief of Scientific Journals of the Maritime University of Szczecin. Area of his research is mainly connected with reliability and safety of technical systems as well as effective maintenance and operation of mechatronics systems. He is especially experienced in machinery operation (engines, power plants, auxiliary machinery, etc) and in modeling of reliability and safety (FTA/RBD/FMEA/FMECA). He is also a Top 500 Innovators member and MIT Enterprise Forum mentor. Apart from that, he is a design thinking and TRIZ leader and developer in Poland. Slawomir Zolkiewski is a visiting professor at Faculty of Mechanical and Power Engineering at Wroclaw University of Science and Technology and an associate professor at Silesian University of Technology in the Institute of Engineering Processes Automation and Integrated Manufacturing Systems. His research interests include automation and robotics, control of mechanical and mechatronic systems, and vision control systems. He has over 100 publications in these areas and was a co-author of several monographs and patents. He participated as a member of organization or scientific committees of many international conferences and is a member of editorial boards of several journals. He also leads laboratories of Robotics in Technological Processes and Composites in Engineering and Operation of Machines. The special issue WorldCIST 2017 includes extended high-quality papers presented at 5th World Conference on Information Systems and Technologies (WorldCIST 2017), Porto Santo Island, Madeira, Portugal, April 11 to 13, 2017, held in conjunction with the Human-Machine Interfaces in Automation, Robotics, Mechanics and Mechatronics (HMInARMM). The main purpose is to provide discussion for people interested in programming environments, computer-human interaction, modeling software, tools, and interfaces. We would like to thank all the authors who submitted valuable contributions to this special issue. We address our special thanks to the general Chair Prof. Alvaro Rocha, all the co-chairs and members of the programme committee of 5th World Conference on Information Systems and Technologies for the feedback provided to this special issue and involvement during the reviewing process. Additionally, we express our special thanks and sincere appreciation to Professor Geoffrey Fox, the Editor in Chief for all the help and guidance enhancing overall quality of the papers and issue.
